segments.s205-g121: شد is the past simple of شدن 'to become' used here as a copular verb; it appears at the start for poetic inversion (more normal prose order would be آن لشکر نامور سوگوار شد). آن modifies لشکر ('that/ the army'), نامور is a pre‑noun adjective 'renowned', and سوگوار is a predicative adjective 'mourning/afflicted' that describes the army as a result of the following causes.
segments.s205-g122: ز is the poetic preposition (like از) meaning 'because of/for' and introduces the cause of the mourning: ز بهرام = 'on account of Bahram'. وز here functions as a fused sequence (و + ز) 'and because of', introducing a second cause; گردش روزگار is a noun phrase 'the turning/changes of time' (گردش modified by روزگار) expressing the vicissitudes of fate.
segments.s205-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از دور is a locative phrase 'from afar' (preposition از + noun دور). گیو is the proper name (object of دیدن). دلیرش is دلیر 'brave' with the enclitic third-person suffix -ش = 'his brave one' (i.e. 'his brave Giv'); بدید is the simple past 'saw' (3rd sg.). Word order: temporal clause 'When ... he saw ...'.
segments.s205-g42: عنان را بپیچید is a past action 'he twisted/turned the reins' (عنان 'reins' + را object marker + بپیچید 'turned/twisted' 3rd sg.). The conjunction و links the next verb. دم درکشید is a verbal phrase 'drew in (his) breath' (دم = 'breath', درکشید = 'drew in/pulled in'); together it describes the simultaneous reaction to seeing Giv.
segments.s205-g43: چو دانست = 'when he knew/realized' (چو = 'when', دانست = past 'knew'). کز introduces a subordinate content clause 'that' (old/poetic form of که). لشکر اندر گذشت is the content clause 'he passed through/among the army' (لشکر 'army', اندر 'in/among', گذشت past 'passed'). The subject is an implied 3rd person throughout.
segments.s205-g44: ز گردان و گردنکشان دور گشت = 'he became distant/withdrew from the troops and the proud/rebellious ones' (ز = 'from', گردان = 'troop/detachment', و = 'and', گردنکشان = 'proud ones/rebels', دور = predicate 'far', گشت = past of گشت 'became/turned' used as copula). Word order places the source phrase before the predicate 'دور گشت'.
segments.s205-g45: سوی او = 'toward him' (سوی 'toward' + pronoun او). بیفکند is a past-tense verb 'he cast/threw' (3rd sg.; classical form with the prefix بی-). پیچان is an adjective 'twisting/winding' that precedes the noun کمند 'lasso/snare'; together 'پیچان کمند' = 'a winding lasso' as the object of بیفکند.
segments.s205-g46: میان تژاو اندر آمد = 'it came into/among Tazhāv' (میان 'amid/in the midst of' + proper name تژاو + اندر آمد 'came in'). به بند is an idiomatic result phrase 'into bondage/into bonds' (به 'into/towards' + بند 'fetter,bond'), so آمد + به بند together communicate 'was taken/caught (in bonds)'.
segments.s205-g47: بران اندر آورد = 'he brought him in/there' (بران is an adverbial 'upon/thereon' or 'on him', اندر آورد 'brought in/dragged in' with آورد past). و برگشت زود = 'and returned/withdrew quickly' (برگشت past 'returned', زود is the adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying برگشت).
segments.s205-g48: پس آسانش از پشت زین در ربود = 'then he easily snatched him from behind the saddle.' پس = 'then/after that'. آسانش is آسان 'easily' with the enclitic -ش = 'him' (so 'him easily'). از پشت زین = 'from the back of the saddle' (از = 'from', پشت = 'behind', زین = 'saddle'). در ربود is an archaic/poetic verb phrase: ربود is past 'seized/snatched' and the prefixed/adjacent در functions here in older Persian to mark removal/entry into a state (literally 'into'/'off'), so the whole means 'snatched off/away.'
segments.s205-g65: چنین (thus) + گفت (said) is a standard opening: "thus he said." با گیو uses archaic با to mark the addressee ("to Giv"). جنگی تژاو is an epithet after the name, describing Giv as a warlike/brilliant figure (جنگی = warlike, تژاو = an epithet meaning bright/beautiful), forming an appositive to گیو.
segments.s205-g66: که introduces the reported comparison/clause (here functioning like “that / (namely)”), تو is the 2nd‑person subject, and چون + عقابی is a simile “like an eagle.” و connects the two halves; من is the speaker “I,” and چون + چکاو gives the contrasting simile “like a lark.” Word order is straightforward subject–predicateless simile: the copula is implied.
segments.s205-g67: ز بهرام بر بد sets the frame “with/regarding Bahram, against (بر) [it was] bad,” and نبردم گمان literally means “I do not hold the opinion / I do not suppose.” Here گمان is a noun (‘opinion/belief’) modifying the whole clause: the speaker denies the belief that he performed poorly against Bahram.
segments.s205-g68: نه negates what precedes (“no / rather”), او را is the definite object (“him”), and the sequence بدست من آمد زمان is an inversion meaning “he did not come into my hand at (that) time” (بدست = into the hand / in the power of; آمد = came; زمان = time). Together: “No — he did not fall into my hands then.”
segments.s205-g69: که here introduces a temporal clause “when.” من چون رسیدم = “when I arrived.” سواران چین is the following nominal phrase “the horsemen of Chin” (چین = China, here denoting forces from Chin). The clause places the speaker’s arrival before the action described next.
segments.s205-g70: ورا is an older form of او را meaning “him” (object). کشته بودند is past perfect/pluperfect “had been killed” (they had killed him). بر دشت کین = “on the plain of vengeance” (بر = on; دشت = plain; کین = vengeance/hatred).
segments.s205-g71: بران بد is a compact idiom: بران = “thereupon/thereon” and بد = “bad/evil,” giving “it was a bad thing” or “ill did follow.” که introduces the reason/explanation: بهرام بیجان شدست = “Bahram became lifeless” (بیجان = lifeless, شدست = archaic third‑person perfect ‘has become / was’).
segments.s205-g72: ز (= از) marks cause “from / because of.” دردش is درد + ش (“his pain/suffering”), and دل گیو is the subject “Giv’s heart.” پیچان is a participial/adjectival form meaning “twisting/writhing,” and شدست again gives the resulting state (“became / was”). Thus: “From his suffering Giv’s heart was twisted.”
segments.s205-g89: گر is the conditional 'if' introducing a subordinate clause. ایدونک is a small poetic/colloquial particle meaning 'even for a moment' or 'even a little'; از وی بمن بد رسید is a typical Persian word order where the agent/source از وی ('from him') precedes the indirect object بمن ('to me') and the adjective بد qualifies the verbal predicate رسید ('reached/befell'); رسید is past tense, third person singular, here meaning 'befell' or 'came to'.
segments.s205-g90: همان روز is a demonstrative phrase 'that same day'; مرگش is 'his death' (مرگ + enclitic possessive -ش). نباید is an impersonal/modal present meaning 'one should not' or 'it ought not to', and چشید is used in the sense 'to witness/experience' (not literal tasting). The clause states a general injunction about witnessing the death on that very day.
segments.s205-g91: سر پر گناهش is a noun phrase 'his head full of sin' where پر is a postpositive adjective meaning 'full (of)' and گناهش is the genitive 'his sin'. روان داد is a compound verbal expression 'gave (his) soul' — past tense, third person singular, idiomatically 'he died' or 'he sent forth his spirit'. The final من is a separate pronoun 'I/me' that connects to the following imperative/optative بمان.
segments.s205-g92: بمان is an imperative or jussive 'remain/let (me) stay'. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that'), and کند is the subjunctive present third person 'he may do / he may make'. در جهان یاد من = 'my remembrance in the world' where یاد is 'remembrance' and من = 'me' (object/genitive). The whole clause expresses purpose: 'Let me remain so that he may cause my memory to exist in the world.'
segments.s205-g93: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو = 'when'. برادر is the subject 'the brother' (here 'the brother' or 'kinsman'), بهرام را uses the object marker را after the proper name, and خسته دید is the verb phrase 'saw (him) tired' — past tense دید 'saw' with predicate adjective خسته following the object marker.
segments.s205-g94: The phrase treats تژاو as a (personal or descriptive) noun followed by a compound epithet جفا پیشه ('one who practices cruelty'); را is the definite object marker attached to that whole phrase. بسته دید is past-tense 'saw (him) bound/tied' (بسته here = 'bound/tied' or 'captured'). Word order is verb-final for the predicate 'saw'.
segments.s205-g95: خروشید is a past-tense verb 'he roared/cried out'. The coordinating و links actions. بگرفت is past tense 'he seized' and takes ریش تژاو as its object: the noun ریش 'beard' followed by the possessor تژاو (genitive placement after the noun), a common Persian order in poetry.
segments.s205-g96: بریدش is past tense 'he cut off/severed it' where the clitic -ش refers back to the head; سر از تن is the idiomatic expression 'head from the body' (از = 'from'). بسان is a simile marker 'like/as', followed by چکاو 'lark' — the simile compares the action to a lark, producing 'cut off his head from his body like a lark.'
segments.s205-g49: بخاک اندر افگند is a compact, somewhat archaic word order: the prepositional phrase بخاک اندر (“onto/to the ground”) comes before the verb افگند (past, “threw/cast”). خوار و نژند are two adjectives following the verb that describe the state of the person cast down (“humiliated and base/lowly”). افگند is the narrative past form.
segments.s205-g50: فرود آمد is a light-verb/compound meaning “he came down” (فرود = descent + آمد = came). The conjunction و links to the next clause. دست کردش به بند is an idiomatic sequence: کردش is κάν (to do) with the 3rd-person object suffix -ش (“him”), and دست here (“hand”) with به بند (“into bonds”) means he took hold of him and bound him — i.e. ‘laid hands on him and fettered him.’
segments.s205-g51: نشست از بر اسپ literally ‘he sat (down) from on the steed’; classical Persian uses نشست از بر + instrument to mean ‘alighted/dismounted from the horse.’ The conjunction و joins the next action. او را کشان is a participial/ablative phrase: را marks او as the definite object and کشان (‘dragging’) describes how he handled him.
segments.s205-g52: پس اندر همی برد is a sequence-marker + spatial phrase + verb: پس ‘then/after’, اندر ‘inside’, همی (an archaic/poetic particle) gives a continuous or immediate colouring (‘he then proceeded to…’), and برد is the narrative past of بردن ‘to take/lead away’ (note: the provided sense list does not show the verbal sense). چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and بیهشان is an archaic adjectival/descrptive element used with چون — here functioning adverbially (‘as if without power/helpless’).
segments.s205-g53: چنین گفت با او is a simple past report: چنین ‘thus/so’ + گفت (past ‘said’) + با او ‘to him.’ بخواهش تژاو is a causative/imperative construction: بخواهش is from بخواه (to ask/command) with the object suffix -ش referring back (or acting as an embedded object), and تژاو is a proper name addressed as the person to be asked/commanded.
segments.s205-g54: که introduces the content/result clause (‘that…’). با من نماند is a jussive/negative-desiderative content: با من ‘with me’ + نماند (subjunctive/negative sense ‘not remain’) expressing that he should not stay with the speaker. The following ای دلیر ایچ تاو are vocatives: ای marks direct address, دلیر is an epithet ‘brave’, and ایچ تاو is a name/epithet addressed to the addressee (here used as a vocative).
segments.s205-g55: چه کردم is an exclamatory/past question ‘what have I done?’ followed by a causal/prepositional phrase کزین (‘from this’) and the noun phrase بی‌شمار انجمن (‘countless assembly/host’), which indicates the group from which some action or treatment has come. The verb of complaint follows in the next segment.
segments.s205-g56: شب تیره دوزخ is a stacked noun phrase: شب تیره ‘dark night’ qualified by the stronger image دوزخ ‘of hell’ (i.e. ‘the dark night of hell’). نمودی is the 2nd-person singular past of نمودن ‘to show’ (‘you showed’), and بمن is the prepositional object (‘to me’). The overall meaning is a complaint that the addressee has made the speaker experience a hellish darkness.
segments.s205-g17: همی is a poetic progressive/adverbial particle meaning a continuing action ('kept / continually'). ریخت is the past stem of ریختن used here intransitively to mean 'poured/flowed'. آب از بر چهراوی is a compact phrase: آب (water/tears) + از (from) + بر (on/over, here forming the idiom 'from the face') + چهراوی (his visage / fair-faced), so literally 'water flowed from his face' — i.e. 'tears poured from his face.' Word order is verb before its adverbial phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s205-g18: پر ز خون = 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'from/of' in poetic register). دو تن دیده is a poetic way to indicate 'the two eyes' / 'both eyes' (دیده = eye), with تن functioning as a corporeal emphatic ('body' / 'person') in the phrase; اوی is the possessive 'his' (here 'his love' follows). از مهر اوی links causally: 'from/because of his love.' The clause therefore states that both eyes were full of blood as a result of his love.
segments.s205-g19: چو + بازآمدش = a temporal clause 'when it/they returned to him' (چو = 'when', بازآمدش = 'returned to him/it came back to him' with enclitic -ش). هوش is 'awareness / consciousness', so چو بازآمدش هوش = 'when his senses returned'. بگشاد (past of گشودن) governs چشم: here it is a past/punctual verb 'opened', so the clause means 'his eyes opened' immediately after his senses returned.
segments.s205-g20: تنش = 'his body' with the possessive enclitic -ش. پر ز خون بود is a full nominal predicate: پر ز خون 'full of blood' + بود past copula 'was'. The coordinate clause و دل پر ز خشم omits the second بود (ellipsis is normal): 'and (his) heart (was) full of anger.' Word order places the possessor enclitic directly on تن and relies on بود for the past-state description.
segments.s205-g21: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said' used as the main verb). با گیو marks the addressee 'to/with Giv' (با = 'to/with' in speech contexts). کای is a vocative particle 'O Kay' (used to address royal names), and نامجوی is a personal epithet or name attached to that vocative. Word order is typical for reported speech: formula + addressee + vocative name.
segments.s205-g22: مرا = 'me' (accusative 'mara' = 'me'). چون introduces an indirect interrogative or manner clause here meaning 'how / when' (چون بپوشی = 'when/how you cover (me)'). بپوشی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of پوشیدن (here instructive/conditional: 'you cover / you shall cover'). بتابوت is the contracted form of به تابوت 'into/to the coffin' (preposition + noun), and روی is 'face' (here indicating orientation: 'with (my) face ...'). The clause is an instruction about how to place/cover the speaker in the coffin.
segments.s205-g23: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. کین here is the object 'vengeance' or imperative goal: 'seek vengeance'. برادر is a vocative 'brother' (address). بخواه is the 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive of خواستن used as a command 'seek / demand'. از تژاو marks the opponent 'against/from Tizhav' (از + name). The whole is an imperative exhortation: 'You, brother, seek vengeance from Tizhav.'
segments.s205-g24: ندارد is the negative present/past-in-context of داشتن 'he does not have / possesses not'. مگر = 'except' (limiting particle). گاو = 'cow' and با = 'with'. شیر here is 'milk' (not 'lion'). تاو is a descriptive noun meaning 'radiance/glow' modifying شیر (i.e. 'milk of radiance' / 'shining milk'). The clause therefore says he has nothing except a cow whose milk is radiant.
segments.s205-g9: بدیدار is a prepositional/nominal phrase (ب + دیدار) meaning 'at the sight/of meeting' or 'on seeing'. بهرامشان is the name بهرام with the third-person plural possessive/enclitic شان ('their Bahram'). بد نیاز is a ready-made adjectival phrase meaning 'badly in need' or 'sorely distressed' (بد modifying نیاز). The whole segment sets the circumstantial time/condition for the following action.
segments.s205-g10: همی is an old/poetic particle giving immediacy or continuative sense before the verb. خسته و کشته are two coordinating adjectival/past‑participle predicates ('weary and slain/wounded') describing the people. جستند is the simple past, 3rd person plural of جستن meaning 'they leapt/sprang' and باز after the verb means 'again'. Together this says they sprang back once more, in the stated exhausted/wounded state.
segments.s205-g11: همه دشت = 'the whole/plain entire plain' (همه here = 'whole, entire'). پرخسته و کشته is a compound/adjectival phrase ('very tired/ exhausted and slain'); these adjectives function as a predicate complement. بود is the past tense copula 'was', so the clause means 'the whole plain was full of the exhausted and the slain.'
segments.s205-g12: جهانی here means 'the whole/world at large' (an expansive subject). بخون اندر آغشته is an older poetic phrasing where بخون = به + خون ('with/covered in blood') and اندر آغشته is a participial phrase 'in/beside soaked' (آغشته = 'soaked/tinged'). بود is again the past copula. The clause literally: 'the world was soaked within/with blood.'
segments.s205-g13: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. دلیران is the plural subject 'the brave men'. بهرام را uses را as the definite object marker to mark بهرام as the object. یافتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they found/they came upon'. The clause is therefore 'when the brave men found Bahram.'
segments.s205-g14: پر از ... = 'full of ...' (پر = 'full', followed by the preposition از expressing 'of'). آب و خون modify دیده ('eyes full of water and blood' — i.e. tears and blood). The verb بشتافتند (3rd pl. past) follows and means 'they hastened/they rushed'. Word order here is adjective/phrase (eyes full of ...) + verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s205-g15: بخاک و بخون is an older/poetic repetition of به + خاک and به + خون = 'into the dust and into blood' (i.e. 'to the ground and in blood'). اندر افگنده is a perfect/passive participle phrase 'cast/laid within' (افگنده = 'cast, thrown down'). خوار is an adjectival predicate 'humbled/despised'. The whole segment describes someone lying cast down and dishonoured.
segments.s205-g16: فتاده is the past participle 'fallen' used predicatively. ازو = 'from him' (enclitic compound of از + او). دست here is literal 'hand' but idiomatically means 'power/ability' — 'his hand had fallen from him' = 'he had lost his strength/hold'. و links to برگشته کار, where برگشته is a past participle 'turned back/reversed' and کار ('affair, deed, power') indicates that the action/enterprise has been undone or turned aside. The sequence is idiomatic/poetic: he lies fallen, his strength gone, and his enterprise reversed.
segments.s205-g57: بزد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb (“he struck/ hit”). The prepositional phrase بر سرش (= “upon his head”) follows the verb; ش is the enclitic possessive “his.” تازیانه دویست is the direct object in inverted, poetic order (noun + numeral) meaning “two hundred lashes/strikes with a whip.”
segments.s205-g58: بدو گفت is literally “to him (بدو) he said”; گفت here functions as the reporting verb (‘he said’), introducing the quoted clause. کین جای گفتار نیست is a verbless nominal clause meaning “this is not a place for speech/words” (جای = place, گفتار = speech, نیست = is not); کین is a poetic/demonstrative or emphatic particle in this context (archaic usage).
segments.s205-g59: ندانی is the negative present/past-subjunctive sense “do you not know” (2nd-person). همی‌ای is an archaic emphatic/vocative cluster (“still/ever” + vocative ای) addressing the addressee; بد شور بخت is a compound epithet: بد = “bad/ill,” شور = “ardor/passion,” and بخت = “fate/fortune,” i.e. “ill‑fated, hot‑hearted (lit. bad‑fated).”
segments.s205-g60: که introduces the content clause “that …”. در باغ کین is a locative phrase “in the garden of vengeance” (کین = vengeance). تازه is an adverb meaning “newly/freshly” placed before the verb in poetic word order. کشتی is a 2nd‑person past/perfect form (‘you …’) in this context (‘you planted/kinds of ‘you did’); درخت is the object “tree.” (Note: کشتی is archaic/poetic and its specific sense is indicated by context.)
segments.s205-g61: که again introduces a relative/descriptive clause. بالاش is بالا + possessive ش (“its top/upper part”). با چرخ همبر بود is an archaic copular expression meaning “was equal/level with the sky” (با = “with/against,” چرخ = “sky/heaven,” همبر = “peer/equal,” بود = past copula “was”).
segments.s205-g62: تنش = تن + ش (“its/his body”). خون خورد is a compact poetic verb phrase meaning literally “ate/drank blood,” i.e. “was blood‑drinking/ blood‑stained” (خورد here functions as the past verb). بار او سر بود is a nominal clause: بار = “burden/load,” او = “his/that one,” سر = “head,” and بود = “was,” so “its burden was his head.”
segments.s205-g63: شکار تو = “your quarry/prey” (possession expressed by the following pronoun تو). The proper name بهرام follows as an apposition: “Bahram (is) your prey.” باید + بجنگ is a necessity construction: باید = “must/ought,” and بجنگ is the subjunctive/infinitive of جنگیدن “to fight,” i.e. “you must fight.”
segments.s205-g64: ببینی is the 2nd‑person present/future form of دیدن (“you will see / you shall see”). کنون = “now.” زخم کام نهنگ is a genitive/noun phrase “the wound of (the) whale” (زخم = wound, کام here functions as an attributive noun meaning desire/mouth/fulfillment in poetic register, and نهنگ = whale), so the clause means “now you will see the whale’s wound.”
segments.s205-g73: کشانش is the present participle/verb stem کشان ‘dragging’ with the third‑person object suffix ‑ش = ‘him’ (i.e. ‘dragging him’). بیارد is an archaic narrative form (classical Persian) corresponding to بیاورد ‘brought/led’ (here past narrative: ‘dragged/brought’). گیو دلیر is the subject in apposition: the proper name Giv followed by the epithet ‘the brave’ (دلیر).
segments.s205-g74: بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘to/into the presence of’ (lit. ‘before’ someone). جگر here is figurative ‘heart/seat of feeling’ (not literal liver) and خسته is a postpositive adjective ‘weary/tired’. بهرام شیر is the name plus epithet (‘Bahram the lion’) with شیر functioning as an honorific epithet. The whole segment means ‘into the presence of Bahram the lion, whose heart was weary.’
segments.s205-g75: بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to him/unto him’ and precedes the verb as is common in classical Persian. گفت is the verb ‘said’ (past); کاینک is an emphatic/formal adverb ‘now’ or ‘at this moment’. سر بی‌وفا literally ‘the head of the faithless’ uses سر (head) metonymically for a person’s life/person — ‘the faithless one’.
segments.s205-g76: مکافات (retribution) is the object; سازم is the 1st‑person present/future form ‘I will make/bring about’ (classical present with م‑). جفا را جفا repeats جفا (‘cruelty/injustice’) with the object marker را before the first instance: a rhetorical doubling meaning ‘retribution — cruelty in return for cruelty.’ را marks the definite object.
segments.s205-g77: سپاس is the noun ‘thanks/praise’ and is followed by the preposition از meaning roughly ‘to/of’ in the formulaic phrase ‘thanks to X’. جهان‌آفرین is a compound adjective/noun ‘world‑creator’ modifying کردگار, which here names God/the Maker. Word order is noun + preposition + modifier + noun.
segments.s205-g78: که introduces a subordinate clause ‘that/for which’. چندان زمان is a quantifying phrase ‘so much time’ (چندان = ‘to that extent’ + زمان ‘time’). دیدم is the past first person ‘I saw’ used idiomatically = ‘I have lived to see’; از روزگار literally ‘of the world/time’ (از + روزگار) indicates the domain/period in which that experience occurred. The clause therefore expresses grateful amazement at having lived through so much of life/time.
segments.s205-g79: که again links the subordinate clause. تیره‌روان is an epithet meaning ‘ill‑fated’ or ‘dark‑souled’ and بداندیش is another epithet ‘ill‑minded/ill‑wishing’. تو here indicates the addressee (‘you’) and sets the scene — these epithets describe the person who will be dealt with in the following clause.
segments.s205-g80: بپردازم is the verb (classical present/subjunctive form) meaning ‘I will attend to / take care of / punish’ (here ‘deal with’). اکنون ‘now’ gives temporal force; من is an emphatic subject pronoun placed after the verb (common in poetry) meaning ‘I’. از پیش تو is a phrase ‘in front of you / in your presence’ (از + پیش + تو), so the whole segment says ‘I will now, before you, attend to/ punish him.’
segments.s205-g97: This clause states the subject and its emotional reaction: دل گیو ('Giv's heart') is a genitive phrase with the possessor after the possessed (typical poetic word order). زان پس ('from that time / thereafter') is a temporal adverbial modifying the verb. بریشان is an archaic compressed form meaning 'for them / toward them' (بر + ایشان) indicating the target of the feeling. بسوخت is a past-tense intransitive verb 'burned' describing the heart's state.
segments.s205-g98: روانش is the noun 'his soul/spirit' with the enclitic possessive -ش ('his'). ز غم ('from/because of grief') is an ablative/causal phrase giving the reason. آتشی is the object or predicate noun 'a fire / a fiery thing' and برفروخت is a past transitive verb 'kindled / inflamed' — together meaning 'his soul kindled a fire because of sorrow.'
segments.s205-g99: خروشی ('a cry / a roar') is the thing produced; برآورد is a past verb here meaning 'brought forth / raised' (classical usage: to produce/utter). کاندر جهان is a locative phrase ('in the world' / 'throughout the world') modifying the verb. Word order places the produced noun before the verb for emphasis, a common poetic device.
segments.s205-g100: که introduces a causal/explanatory subordinate clause here ('for / because'). دید is the past verb 'he saw / beheld.' این شگفت is the direct object 'this marvel/wonder.' آشکار و نهان are two adjectives linked by و ('and'), meaning 'manifest and hidden' — they qualify the marvel and complete the reason for the cry.
segments.s205-g101: This is the first (parallel) protasis: که‌گر ('if') opens a conditional. من کشم and ور کشی are two parallel verb phrases with first- and second-person forms (کشم, کشی) used as subjunctive/conditional in classical Persian — the object is omitted and understood from context. ور ('if') repeats the conditional alternative; پیش من ('before me / in my presence') is a locative phrase attached to the second alternative.
segments.s205-g102: This is the apodosis and a subordinated conditional: برادر بود‌گر is an inverted order meaning 'he would be a brother if ...' (بود‌گر = 'would be if'). کسی is the indefinite subject of the subordinate clause, and خویش من ('my own / my kinsman') expresses the condition (خویش here formed with root خوی + enclitic ش in the manuscript tradition yields 'own/kinsman'). The clause as a whole states a hypothetical social relation contingent on kinship.
segments.s205-g103: بگفت این ('he said this') is a straightforward past-tense report: بگفت = 'he said' with این as its object. و ('and') links the next clause. بهرام یل is a nominal apposition ('Bahram, the hero'). جان بداد is an idiomatic poetic phrase: جان ('life') plus بداد (here used as a past of دادن, 'gave') meaning 'gave up his life' (i.e., died/sacrificed).
segments.s205-g104: جهان را uses را as the definite object marker ('the world' as object). چنین است ساز ونهاد is a compressed poetic clause: چنین است ('thus is / thus it is') followed by ساز ونهاد — two verbs in classical narrative sense meaning 'he fashioned/made (ساز) and placed/established (نهاد)'. Taken together: 'thus he fashioned and established the world.' The subject is the previously mentioned actor.
segments.s205-g33: This short nominal phrase + verb means 'X swore an oath'. بدادار دارنده are two nouns in apposition (epithetic/title + holder/possessor) functioning as the subject; سوگند is the object 'an oath'; خورد is the past tense verb literally 'ate' but here part of the idiom سوگند خورد 'to take/swore an oath'. Word order is Subject + Object + Verb (typical Persian).
segments.s205-g34: A poetic adverbial phrase specifying the oath's solemnity: بروز 'by/at the day', سپید 'white/bright' qualifies it; و links the two parallel phrases; شب 'night' qualified by لاژورد 'lapis‑blue/ultramarine'. The whole phrase is an ablative/adjunct to سوگند خورد ('swore by ...').
segments.s205-g35: که introduces a content clause ('that...'). جز ... نبیند is a negative-except construction meaning 'shall see nothing except'; ترگ رومی is the excepted object 'a Roman rag'; سرم is the possessor/subject 'my head' (idiomatically 'I' or 'my life' — Persian often uses سرم for the experiencer). Word order is Relative/Complementizer + exception phrase + verb + possessor.
segments.s205-g36: مگر here means 'unless' (introducing the exception/condition). کین 'vengeance' is the object; بهرام is the possessor/name; بازآورم is a present/future‑oriented 1st person singular verb 'I will bring back / I will recover' — syntactically this clause supplies the condition: 'unless I bring back Bahram's vengeance.'
segments.s205-g37: پر از درد و پر کین is a parallel adjectival phrase 'full of pain and full of vengeance' (پر + از + noun / repeated پر + noun). بزین برنشست is a compound verbal action: بزین (saddle/onto the saddle) + برنشست 'sat upon' — together meaning 'mounted (his horse)'. The clause describes his state (emotional) and the action of mounting.
segments.s205-g38: یکی is an indefinite 'one' / 'a certain' referring back to the actor; تیغ هندی 'Indian sword' is the direct object with هندی as an adjectival qualifier; گرفته is a perfect/passive participle used in the sense 'taken/seized'; بدست is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in (his) hand'. The whole phrase means 'he took an Indian sword in his hand.'
segments.s205-g39: بدانگه 'thereupon/at that moment' is an adverbial connector; که introduces the clause that follows. شد is the past of 'to become' and combines with روی گیتی سیاه 'the face of the world (became) black' — subject 'روی گیتی' followed by predicate adjective 'سیاه'. The sequence shows a change of state.
segments.s205-g40: تژاو 'radiance/brightness' is the subject; از طلایه 'from the vanguard' marks source; برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth'; براه 'on the road / along the way' is a locative/ directional adjunct. The clause describes light issuing from the vanguard as they set out on the road.
segments.s205-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical usage introducing a temporal clause). خورشید تابنده is a noun + adjective phrase 'the shining sun'. بنمود is a past‑tense verb (3rd sg. of بنمودن) 'appeared/showed'. پشت is the noun 'back' (here the object/phrase completing 'benamud' — idiomatically 'the sun showed itself' or 'the sun appeared'). Word order is temporal clause (چو + subject + verb + complement).
segments.s205-g2: دل گیو گشت is a small nominal clause: دل (heart) with possessor گیو, then گشت (past of گشتن) used as 'became/turned'. از introduces the target/relation ('with respect to' / 'toward' his brother). برادر is the reference 'brother'. درشت is an adjective 'harsh/rough' qualifying the feeling; overall 'Giv's heart became harsh toward his brother.'
segments.s205-g3: ببیژن is the prepositional phrase 'to Bijan' (به + بیژن contracted) indicating the addressee. چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت here functions as the speech verb). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and رهنمای is the vocative noun 'guide' — together 'O guide'. The clause is an instance of direct speech introduction.
segments.s205-g4: برادر نیامد is a negated past statement 'the brother did not come/return' (نیامد = past negative of آمدن). همی is an archaic adverb ('again' / 'still' / emphatic here supporting باز). باز modifies آمدن as 'back/again' (باز = 'again/return'), and جای is 'place' (meaning his proper position/place). Word order is subject + negated verb + adjuncts.
segments.s205-g5: بباید شدن is an impersonal obligation construction: بباید ('it is necessary' / 'one must') + شدن (infinitive/verb) gives 'one must go / it is necessary to proceed'. تا is the purpose marker 'so that / in order that'. وراکار (وراه + کار or ورا + کار) means 'his affairs/business there' (وراکار = 'the business there'). چیست (چی + ست) literally 'what is' — a question/nominal clause 'what it is'. Together: 'one must (go) to find out what his business there is.'
segments.s205-g6: نباید که بر رفته is a comment clause: نباید که 'it is not fitting/should not be that' + بر رفته (compound with رفته 'gone/departed') meaning 'that he has gone'. The following باید گریست is a contrasting obligation: 'one must weep' (باید = 'one must', گریست = subjunctive/infinitive form meaning 'to weep' or 'weep!'). The sentence thus balances disapproval ('it is not fitting that he has gone') with the demand to lament.
segments.s205-g7: دلیران برفتند is straightforward plural past 'the brave ones went/departed' (دلیران = 'brave men', برفتند = past 3rd pl. of رفتن). هر دو 'both' follows to emphasize that both of them acted. چو گرد is a simile 'like a گرد'; here چو is 'like/as' and گرد evokes a whirling motion ('as a whirlwind' / 'like dust'), so the phrase means 'they moved like a whirlwind.'
segments.s205-g8: بدان جای is a prepositional phrase 'to/into that place' (ب + دان = به + آن → 'to that'). جای = 'place'. پرخاش و ننگ و نبرد is a list of nouns joined by و: پرخاش (assault/aggression), ننگ (shame/disgrace), نبرد (battle) — together describing the character of the place they went toward. The whole segment expresses movement into a place characterized by attack, shame, and combat.
segments.s205-g81: همی کرد is a classical narrative/progressive phrasing: همی (iterative/continuous marker) + کرد (past of کردن) yields “kept doing / kept making.” خواهش is the noun “request/entreaty” and here is the direct object of کرد. بریشان is the contracted preposition بر + ایشان = “to/for them” (indicating the addressees of the pleading). تژاو (etymologically a word meaning ‘radiance’) functions here as a proper-name or the thing being asked for (i.e. the beloved) and follows the verb phrase as the requested object.
segments.s205-g82: همی خواست is again iterative: “kept wishing/longing.” از in this position means “because of / on account of.” کشتن is the infinitive “to kill,” and خوی + ش is a possessive cluster (خویش = “one’s own” / “himself”) with ش as the 3rd-person enclitic “his.” تاو (= the same radiant-name) names the cause/object of his wish: he wants to kill himself on account of her.
segments.s205-g83: همی گفت is a narrative past (همی marking the reported action + گفت ‘he said’). ار is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle strengthening the conditional (roughly “indeed/if so”); این کار بود is a simple past copular clause “this matter would be/is so” (بود here as past/copula describing the hypothetical).
segments.s205-g84: سر من is possession expressed with the possessed before the possessor (classical order) = “my head.” بخنجر is the instrumental/prepositional phrase ‘with a dagger’ (بـ + خنجر contracted), and بریدن is the infinitive “to cut / behead.” چه سود is a rhetorical question “what profit/what good (would it be)?” expressing that such suicide would be pointless.
segments.s205-g85: یکی here is a modal/indefinite particle meaning “let / if only / perhaps” or “one (as a humble supposition).” بنده باشم uses بنده (servant) + باشم (1st-person subjunctive of بودن) to express an optative/wish: “may I be a servant” / “let me be your servant.” روان ترا has روان “soul/spirit” followed by the 2nd-person enclitic ت (your) and the object marker را (here written as ترا) so it reads “your soul” as the object of the following verb.
segments.s205-g86: پرستش کنم is the noun پرستش “worship” + کنم (1st-person subjunctive/optative of کردن) giving “that I worship / I will worship.” گوربان is an exclamatory idiom meaning literally “I would be buried (for you),” i.e. “I sacrifice myself for you” (an expression of extreme devotion). ترا again marks the addressee (you) with the object marker.
segments.s205-g87: چنین گفت is the standard narrative formula “thus said.” با here introduces the addressee (“to/with”) — classical texts often use با + name to indicate the person being spoken to. گیو بهرام شیر are personal names/titles (Giv and Bahram, with شیر ‘lion’ as an epithet).
segments.s205-g88: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/attention particle (classical poetic address, roughly 'O ...'). The three following adjectives نامور, نامدار, دلیر are piled epithets in apposition: all are adjectives praising the addressee — “renowned / illustrious / brave.”
segments.s205-g105: عنان بزرگی is a genitive noun phrase where عنان (literally 'reins/bridle') stands metaphorically for control or power, followed by بزرگی 'greatness'. هرآنکو بجست is a relative/conditional clause: هرآنکو = 'whoever' and بجست is a past-tense verb 'sought'. The verb comes after the relativizer in this old/poetic word order.
segments.s205-g106: نخستین 'first' heads the following necessity clause. بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (present necessity). بخون here is an archaic contraction for به خون 'with/in blood' (poetic usage), and دست شست is a compound in which شست is the past tense verb 'washed' taking دست 'hand' as its object (i.e. 'wash one's hand in blood'). The whole segment gives the obligation: one must first wash one's hands in blood.
segments.s205-g107: This is a paired conditional: اگر introduces 'if', خود is reflexive/emphatic 'himself', and the sequence is to be read as اگر خود کشد / گر کشندش — 'if he kills himself, or if they kill him'. کشد (embedded in the compound token here) is a third-person verb 'kills' in the protasis, and کشندش is 'they kill him' (کشند + object suffix -ش). بدرد is a predicate meaning 'to be of use' (here understood in context as 'of no avail' or 'it will not help').
segments.s205-g108: بگرد is an imperative meaning 'go about/wander' or 'search', here used as an admonition. جهان is its object 'the world'. تا توانی is a limiting clause 'as long/as far as you can' (تا = 'as far as/so that', توانی = 'you are able'). مگرد is a prohibitive 'do not turn back / do not return' (poetic negative imperative).
segments.s205-g109: خروشان is an adverbial or adjectival participle 'roaring/with a roar'. بر اسپ is the prepositional phrase 'upon the steed/horse'. تژاوش (from تژاو) here identifies the steed or its radiance (a poetic name/epithet). ببست is a past tense verb 'he bound/tied' and takes the preceding phrase as its object/location.
segments.s205-g110: به بیژن سپرد is a simple past clause 'he entrusted (it/him) to Bijan' (به + name + past verb سپرد). آنگهی 'then/thereupon' links to the next action, and برنشست is past 'he mounted/sat upon' (بر + نشست). The sequence gives three consecutive actions.
segments.s205-g111: بیاوردش is the past-tense verb 'he brought' with the object clitic -ش 'him'. از جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the place/station', and تژاو names that place or its epithet (lit. 'radiance') — together 'from Tazhav's place'.
segments.s205-g112: بنزدیک ایران is a locative phrase 'near/close to Iran' (بنزدیک = 'near'). دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive suffix -ش). پر ز تاو is a predicative expression 'full of radiance' (پر = full (of) + ز = 'of/from' + تاو = 'radiance/glow'), so 'his heart was full of that radiance.'
segments.s205-g113: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. شد is the past of شدن 'to become/get' and here combines with دور (predicate adjective) to mean 'became far' = 'moved away'. زان is a prepositional/relational particle 'from that', and جایگاه نبرد is a genitive noun phrase 'the place of battle' (جایگاه 'place' modified by نبرد 'battle'). Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: temporal particle + verb + predicate + prepositional phrase specifying source.
segments.s205-g114: بکردار is an adjectival/qualifying form (literally 'in good/proper manner' or 'well‑formed'); it qualifies the following noun phrase. ایوان 'iwan/porch' names the location; یکی 'one' is the numeral/indefinite 'a single'; دخمه 'vault/tomb chamber' is the direct object; کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/built'. The sequence places the descriptive/locative phrase before the verb, so the whole reads 'he made/built one chamber in the iwan, in proper fashion.'
segments.s205-g115: بیاگند here functions as a verb meaning 'they anointed/scented' (an embalming action) applied to مغزش 'his brain' (مغز + possessive -ش). بمشک is a prepositional/ instrumental phrase 'with musk' and و connects the next aromatic noun عبیر. In this poetic register the verb covers the act of treating the brain with aromatic substances.
segments.s205-g116: تنش ('his body' = تن + enclitic -ش) is marked by the object marker را, placing it before the verb. بپوشید is the past form of پوشیدن used here as 'they covered/wrapped/clothed'. چینی حریر is a noun + adjective phrase 'Chinese silk' specifying the material used. Order: object (with را) + verb + material phrase.
segments.s205-g117: برآیین is a frozen prepositional phrase 'according to the custom/manner of'; شاهانش is 'of the kings' (شاهان + enclitic -ش) indicating whose custom; بر introduces the locative phrase تخت عاج 'on the ivory throne/bed' (تخت 'throne/bed', عاج 'ivory'). The whole prepositional cluster sets the manner and place for the following verb.
segments.s205-g118: بخوابید is past tense of خوابیدن 'he slept' but in epic/poetic usage can be a euphemism for 'was laid to rest' or 'died'. و 'and' links the clauses. آویخت is past tense 'hung' (active or stative), followed by the prepositional phrase بر سرش تاج 'a crown on/over his head' (سر + possessive -ش). The verb governs the following prepositional object, so 'a crown hung over his head.'
segments.s205-g119: سر here means 'top' or 'head' and is linked with دخمه 'tomb' (i.e., 'the tomb's top/head'). کردند is the past plural 'they made/did', and سرخ و کبود are two color adjectives 'red and dark-blue/dusky' describing what they made it. The sequence is subject implied (they) + object phrase + verb + adjectives as result.
segments.s205-g120: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. گفتی is the past 2nd‑person 'you said' (here introducing reported speech) and is followed by the complementizer که 'that'. بهرام is the proper name 'Bahram'. هرگز 'never' modifies نبود, which is the past negative of بودن 'to be' meaning 'was not' or 'was never (there)'. Word order: narrator's 'you said that' + subordinate clause 'Bahram was never (there)'.
segments.s205-g25: مرا (م + را) is the object 'me' with the object marker را attached; placing the object before the verb gives emphasis. دید is the simple past 'saw/beheld.' پیران ویسه is the subject (a proper name/title: Piran Viseh). نخست is an adverb 'at first/initially' modifying the action.
segments.s205-g26: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'پیران ویسه' ('who/that'). با من = 'with me' (با + object pronoun). بدش روزگاری نشست is an idiomatic poetic clause: بد = 'bad/unfavorable', ش = possessive clitic 'his', روزگاری = 'time/fate', نشست = past of 'نشستن' literally 'sat' but used idiomatically 'a bad fate sat (upon him)' = 'he was in bad times/was afflicted by misfortune.'
segments.s205-g27: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone.' نامداران = 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (a noun of persons). و links the two groups. گردان چین = 'the battalions/hosts of Chin' (گردان 'troop/host', چین = 'Chin/China' here as a geographic or poetic reference).
segments.s205-g28: بجستند is past plural of بجستن 'to spring/leap/seek' — here 'they rushed/sprang.' The phrase با من marks the target 'upon me.' بغاز کین is a genitive/compound: بغاز 'to the throat/neck (figuratively: to seize one's throat)' modified by کین 'vengeance/hatred' — an idiom meaning 'to attack in deadly vengeance.'
segments.s205-g29: تن من is 'my body' (noun followed by the pronoun for possession). تژاو is a poetic epithet placed after the possessor (here functioning adjectivally: 'radiant/bright' in the lexicon provided). جفاپیشه ('جفا پیشه') is an epithet 'one given to cruelty/oppression.' خست is the past-state adjective/verb 'was/grew weary' — the clause says the speaker's body became exhausted under cruel treatment.
segments.s205-g30: نکرد is past negative 'did not do' (here 'did not [give/leave/remember]'). ایچ is an emphatic negative 'nothing/at all.' یاد = 'remembrance/memory.' از introduces the domain 'of/regarding.' نژاد = 'lineage/ancestry,' و = 'and,' نشست = 'meeting/session/encounter' (here 'our meeting'). Together: 'he did not at all remember any memory of my lineage or our meeting.'
segments.s205-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بهرام is the subject 'Bahram.' گرد before 'این سخن' functions adverbially/postpositionally as 'about/regarding' (poetic usage). یاد کرد = 'remembered/recalled' (کرد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb used with یاد).
segments.s205-g32: ببارید is an intransitive past 'fell/was shed' (used for drops/tears). گیو is the subject (Giv). از مژه = 'from the eyelash(es)' (ablative/source), آب زرد = 'yellow/pale water' — a conventional poetic expression for tears, where زرد qualifies آب.

sentences.s205-s61: That famed army became mournful for Bahram and for the vicissitudes of fate.
sentences.s205-s21: When he saw Giv, his brave man, from afar, he twisted the reins and drew in his breath.
sentences.s205-s22: When he realized that he had passed through the army, he drew away from the detachments and the proud fighters.
sentences.s205-s23: He cast toward him a twisting lasso; it came among Tazhāv and caught him in bonds.
sentences.s205-s24: He hauled him in and quickly withdrew; then, easily, he snatched him off the back of the saddle.
sentences.s205-s33: Thus he said to Giv, the warlike Tazhāv: “You are like an eagle and I like a lark.”
sentences.s205-s34: I do not suppose I fared badly against Bahram — no, he did not come into my hands at that time.
sentences.s205-s35: For when I arrived, the horsemen of Chin had slain him on the plain of vengeance.
sentences.s205-s36: It was ill indeed: Bahram lay lifeless; from his suffering Giv’s heart was twisted.
sentences.s205-s45: If even a little bad thing should come to me from him, one should not witness his death on that very day.
sentences.s205-s46: He gave up his soul with his head full of sin; let me remain so that he may keep my memory in the world.
sentences.s205-s47: When the brother saw Bahram weary, he saw Tazhāv, the cruelty-practitioner, bound.
sentences.s205-s48: He roared and seized Tazhāv's beard; he cut off his head from his body like a lark.
sentences.s205-s25: He cast him to the ground, humiliated and lowly; he came down and laid hands on him, putting him in fetters.
sentences.s205-s26: He dismounted from the steed and, dragging him, then led him inside as if he were powerless.
sentences.s205-s27: Thus he said to him, “Ask Tazhav that he not remain with me, O brave Ich-Tav!”
sentences.s205-s28: What have I done that from this countless assembly you have shown me the dark night of hell?
sentences.s205-s9: Tears of water kept pouring from his face; both his eyes were full of blood because of his love for him.
sentences.s205-s10: When his senses returned his eyes opened; his body was full of blood and his heart full of anger.
sentences.s205-s11: Thus he said to Giv, 'O Kay Namjuy, ...' (introducing the speech).
sentences.s205-s12: You — seek vengeance, brother, against Tizhav; he has nothing except a cow with shining milk.
sentences.s205-s5: At the sight of their Bahram, sorely distressed, they leapt back again, weary and slain.
sentences.s205-s6: The whole plain was full of the weary and the slain; the world was soaked in blood.
sentences.s205-s7: When the brave men found Bahram, they hurried — their eyes full of water and blood.
sentences.s205-s8: He lay cast into dust and blood, despised; his hand had fallen from him and his power was reversed/undone.
sentences.s205-s29: He struck him on the head with two hundred lashes, and said to him, “This is no place for words.”
sentences.s205-s30: Do you not know, O ill‑fated, hot‑hearted one, that in the garden of vengeance you have newly planted a tree?
sentences.s205-s31: That (tree) whose top was on a level with the sky, its trunk drank blood, and its burden was his head.
sentences.s205-s32: Bahram is your quarry; you must fight. Now you will see the wound of the whale.
sentences.s205-s37: The brave Giv dragged him forward to Bahram the lion, whose heart was weary.
sentences.s205-s38: He said to him, “Now I will make the faithless one pay — retribution: cruelty for cruelty.”
sentences.s205-s39: Thanks to the Creator of the world, I have lived to see so much of life.
sentences.s205-s40: that I may now, in your presence, deal with the ill‑fated, ill‑minded one.
sentences.s205-s49: From that time Giv's heart burned for them, and his soul, because of sorrow, kindled a fire.
sentences.s205-s50: He raised a cry throughout the world, for he saw this marvel, manifest and hidden.
sentences.s205-s51: If I slay (him), or if you slay (him) before me, he would be a brother — if he were my kinsman.
sentences.s205-s52: He said this, and Bahram the hero gave up his life; thus he fashioned and established the world.
sentences.s205-s17: The oppressor, the possessor, swore an oath by the white day and the lapis‑blue night.
sentences.s205-s18: That my head shall see nothing but a Roman rag, unless I retrieve Bahram's vengeance.
sentences.s205-s19: Filled with pain and full of vengeance, he mounted (his steed); he took an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s205-s20: Thereupon the face of the world grew dark, and a radiance from the vanguard rose along the road.
sentences.s205-s1: When the shining sun appeared, Giv's heart turned harsh toward his brother.
sentences.s205-s2: He said to Bijan thus: 'O guide, the brother did not return to his place.'
sentences.s205-s3: One must go to see what business he has there; it is not fitting that he has gone — one must weep.
sentences.s205-s4: The brave ones both departed like a whirlwind to that place of assault, shame, and battle.
sentences.s205-s41: He kept begging them for Tazhāv; he continually wanted to kill himself on her account.
sentences.s205-s42: He said, 'If that were so, what good would it do to cut off my head with a dagger?'
sentences.s205-s43: ‘Let me be a humble servant; I will worship your spirit, I would be sacrificed for you.’
sentences.s205-s44: Thus he said to Giv (Bahrām-Shīr): 'O renowned, illustrious, brave one.'
sentences.s205-s53: Whoever seeks the reins of greatness must first wash his hands in blood.
sentences.s205-s54: If he kills himself, or if they kill him, it will do him no good; roam the world as far as you can, do not turn back.
sentences.s205-s55: He bound (it) upon the steed Tazhav with a roar, entrusted (him) to Bijan, and then mounted.
sentences.s205-s56: He brought him from Tazhav’s station to near Iran; his heart was full of that radiance.
sentences.s205-s57: When he had moved away from that place of battle, he built a single vaulted chamber in the iwan in proper fashion.
sentences.s205-s58: They anointed his brain with musk and ambergris, and they wrapped his body in Chinese silk.
sentences.s205-s59: According to the custom of kings he lay upon an ivory throne, and a crown hung above his head.
sentences.s205-s60: They made the top of the tomb red and blue; you said that Bahram was never there.
sentences.s205-s13: Piran Viseh first saw me, who sat with me in a time of misfortune.
sentences.s205-s14: All the famed men and the battalions of Chin sprang upon me in an onslaught of vengeance.
sentences.s205-s15: My body was worn out; by cruel oppression I grew weary — he did not at all remember my lineage or our meeting.
sentences.s205-s16: When Bahram recalled these words, Giv let fall from his eyelashes pale-yellow water (i.e., shed tears).
